您可能已经注意到，PurpleAir和AirNow.gov为您的邮政编码提供了不同的AQI值。例如，现在，AirNow.gov上给出的伯克利94720邮政编码的空气质量指数是107.。
在布雷特·辛格(劳伦斯·伯克利实验室的空气质量科学家)、阿里安·戴布瓦德(紫色空气公司的首席执行官)和美国环保署的米歇尔·韦兰的帮助下，我能够准确地拼凑出正在发生的事情。
首先，让我们讨论一下LRAPA转换。正如https://www.lrapa.org/307/Air-Quality-Sensors，中所讨论的，经验研究发现，在测量木材烟雾时，PurpleAir监控器往往比监管机构的质量监控器(如AirNow所使用的质量监控器)产生更大的AQI值(有关原因，请参阅本文末尾)。为了纠正这一点，莱恩地区空气保护局开发了一种内置在紫色空气中的转换公式。
要应用LRAPA公式，请单击单词“None”并将其更改为“LRAPA”。执行此操作时，您将看到与AirNow处于同一级别的数字。如果没有这个换算因子，你就是在把苹果比作橙子。
在这一点上，我们可以相信紫色空气号码会给出与AirNow相同类型的号码。然而，您可能已经注意到，AirNow提供的AQI是107，而几乎所有这些值都在七八十年代。
这个问题比这里出现的还要糟糕。如果我们尝试远至94804(里士满)、94720(伯克利)、94578(圣莱安德罗)或94570(莫拉加)的邮政编码，您会看到我们得到的AQI完全相同：
但是，查看启用了LRAPA的PurpleAir地图，我们看到这一巨大的~100平方英里区域的AQI是高度可变的，在Orinda的值为150+，但在Berkeley只有80左右：
原来，在AirNow.gov上点击“我附近的监视器”，我们可以在地图上看到“奥克兰报告区”中的四个传感器：
这些传感器位于伯克利水上公园(AQI：104)、西奥克兰(AQI：无数据)、莱尼学院(AQI：107)和东奥克兰(AQI：67)。
请注意，莱尼学院的AQI与我们获得的报告区内所有邮政编码的AQI完全相同。这不是意外。AirNow的首页只返回与您的邮政编码对应的报告区域中传感器的最大值。
在某些情况下，如果您只使用首页编号而不查看地图，这可能会导致非常不准确的结果。例如，如果您输入里士满点邮政编码(94801)，则该邮政编码位于圣拉斐尔报告区。
该区域只有一个位于圣拉斐尔的传感器，因此，尽管里士满点在紫色空气上的AQI约为110，但AirNow.gov为您提供了圣拉斐尔传感器的AQI(60年代中期)。
简而言之：我不会使用AirNow的头版号码。相反，您可以使用AirNow地图并找到离您最近的传感器，或者使用启用了LRAPA转换因子的PurpleAir。
还有最后一个重要的现象在烟雾事件中值得一提，比如现在的空气质量指数每小时都有很大的变化。
早些时候，我们看到AirNow东奥克兰传感器的AQI低得出奇，只有67：
相比之下，紫色空气公司的空气质量指数(AQI)更接近110：
在本例中，出现差异的原因有两个：一个是AirNow数字是过去一个小时的平均读数(我不是100%确定，但我非常确定)，而PurpleAir的默认读数是过去10分钟的平均读数。另一个更重要的原因是，AirNow的数字并不是实时的，而是延迟了长达3个小时。如果您查看AirNow地图上的时间戳，您会发现它使用的是在上午9点最后更新的数据，与在上午10点09分更新的PurpleAir进行了比较。
如果我们点击看起来最接近AirNow的东奥克兰传感器的传感器，我们会看到，就在上午9点之前，AQI出现了一个峰值，在这个峰值之前，AQI在60度左右，正如AirNow.gov的地图所报道的那样。AirNow地图刚刚过时。
注意：您还可以将PurpleAir更改为“1小时平均”，而不是“10分钟平均”，但在我看来，这破坏了PurpleAir的优势之一，即它允许您查看实时数据。
一句话：如果你想要最新的AQI测量，我会推荐使用启用了LRAPA的紫色空气。如果您决定使用AirNow.gov，请使用地图查找最近的传感器，而不是首页的值，并注意数据可能会在1-3小时后过期。
如果您不喜欢使用AirNow.gov的地图，但仍想要来自其更高质量传感器的数据(例如，因为您需要使用官方政府数据作为设施点的管理员)，您还可以在https://www.baaqmd.gov/about-air-quality/current-air-quality/air-monitoring-data/#/aqi-highs上以表格形式查看当前数据。
2.。伦敦政治经济学院的伍迪·德尔普和布雷特·辛格所著的“低成本家庭空气质量监测器证明对野火烟雾有用”：https://newscenter.lbl.gov/2020/08/18/low-cost-home-air-quality-monitors-prove-useful-for-wildfire-smoke/.。再次感谢布雷特·辛格提供有关LRAPA的信息。
3.紫色空气公司使用的激光传感器依赖于一个硬编码常数，该常数代表它正在探测的粒子的平均密度。由于木材烟雾颗粒的密度(1.5g/cm^3)低于典型的PM2.5颗粒，因此产生的AQI值过高。这就是为什么需要进行LRAPA转换的原因。多亏了艾德里安·迪布瓦德。
note_en:
You may have noticed PurpleAir and AirNow.gov giving different AQI values for your zip code. For example, right now, the AQI given on AirNow.gov for the 94720 zip code of Berkeley is 107.
    With the help of Brett Singer (an air quality scientist at Lawrence Berkeley Labs), Arian Dybwad (CEO of Purple Air), and Michelle Wayland at the EPA, I was able to piece together exactly what’s going on.
  First, let’s discuss LRAPA conversion. As discussed in  https://www.lrapa.org/307/Air-Quality-Sensors, Empirical studies have found that PurpleAir monitors tend to yield larger AQI values than regulatory agency quality monitors like those used by AirNow (see end of this article for more on why) when measuring wood smoke. To correct for this, the Lane Regional Air Protection Agency has developed a conversion formula that is built into Purple Air.
 To apply the LRAPA formula, click the word “None” and change it to “LRAPA”. When you do this, you’ll see numbers that are on the same scale as AirNow. Without this conversion factor, you are comparing apples to orange.
    At this point, we can trust the Purple Air numbers to be giving the same type of number as AirNow. However, you may have noticed that the AQI that AirNow was giving was 107, whereas almost all of these values are in the 70s and 80s.
 This issue is even worse than it appears here. If we try zip codes as far flung as 94804 (Richmond), 94720 (Berkeley), 94578 (San Leandro), or 94570 (Moraga), you’ll see that we get the exact same AQI of 107:
    But looking at the PurpleAir map with LRAPA enabled, we see that the AQI across this huge ~100 square mile region is highly variable, with values of 150+ in Orinda, but only 80 or so in Berkeley:
  It turns out there are four sensors in the “Oakland Reporting Area” that we can see on a map by clicking “Monitors Near Me” on AirNow.gov:
  These sensors are at Berkeley Aquatic Park (AQI: 104), West Oakland (AQI: No Data Available), Laney College (AQI: 107), and East Oakland (AQI: 67).
 Note that the Laney College AQI is exactly the same as we got for all zip codes in the reporting area. This is no accident.  The front page of AirNow simply returns the max of the sensors in the reporting area corresponding to your zip code.
 In some cases, this can lead to pretty inaccurate results if you just use the front page numbers without looking at the map. For example, if you enter the Point Richmond zip code (94801), this is in the San Rafael reporting area.
  This area consists of a single sensor in San Rafael, so even though Point Richmond has an AQI of around 110 on Purple Air, AirNow.gov provides you with the AQI for the San Rafael sensor (in the mid 60s).
  In short: I wouldn’t use the AirNow front page number. Instead, either use the AirNow map and find the sensor closest to you, or use PurpleAir with the LRAPA conversion factor enabled.
  There’s one last important phenomenon that is worth mentioning during a smoke event like the current moment where AQI varies dramatically from hour to hour.
 Earlier, we saw that the AirNow east oakland sensor had a strangely low AQI of 67:
  This is compared to Purple Air which would suggest an AQI of more like 110:
  In this case, there are two reasons for the discrepancy:  One is that the AirNow numbers are the average reading over the last hour (I’m not 100% certain on this, but I’m pretty sure), whereas the default on PurpleAir is to average over the last 10 minutes. The other more important reason is that the AirNow numbers are not real time, but are instead delayed by up to 3 hours. If you look at the time stamp on the AirNow map, you’ll see that it is using data that was last updated at 9 AM, compared the PurpleAir which was updated at 10:09 AM.
 If we click on the sensor that looks closest to AirNow’s East Oakland sensor, we see that right before 9 AM, there was a spike in AQI, and before this spike, AQI was in the 60s, as reported by AirNow.gov’s map. The AirNow map is just out of date.
  Note: You can also change PurpleAir to give “One Hour Average” instead of “10 Minute Average”, but in my opinion this defeats one of the advantages of PurpleAir, namely that it lets you see real time data.
 Bottom line: If you want the most up to date AQI measurements, I’d recommend using Purple Air with LRAPA enabled. If you decide to use AirNow.gov, use the map to find your closest sensor instead of the front page value, and be aware that the data may be out of date by 1–3 hours.
  If you don’t like using AirNow.gov’s map but still want the data from their higher quality sensors (for example, because you are required to use official government data as the administrator of a facility), you can also see the current data in tabular form at  https://www.baaqmd.gov/about-air-quality/current-air-quality/air-monitoring-data/#/aqi-highs
 2. “Low-Cost Home Air Quality Monitors Prove Useful for Wildfire Smoke” by Woody Delp and Brett Singer at LBL:  https://newscenter.lbl.gov/2020/08/18/low-cost-home-air-quality-monitors-prove-useful-for-wildfire-smoke/. Thanks again to Brett Singer for info on LRAPA.
 3. The laser sensors used by Purple Air rely on a hard-coded constant that represents the average density of the particles it is detecting. Because wood smoke particles are less dense (1.5 g/cm^3) than typical PM 2.5 particles, the resulting AQI values are too high. This is the reason that LRAPA conversion is necessary. Thanks to Adrian Dybwad.
title: 了解PurpleAir与AirNoow.gov对木材烟雾污染的测量
title_en: Understanding PurpleAir vs. AirNow.gov Measurements of Wood Smoke Pollution
